rows.question: What does Hildy plan to do after leaving the newspaper, and with whom?

cutroom.answer:
All three frames verified — the office confrontation between Hildy and Walter (frames 1 & 2) and the restaurant scene with Walter and Bruce (frame 3). Here is the fully sourced answer.

---

## What Hildy Plans to Do After Leaving the Newspaper

### 1. Quit the newspaper entirely and live a "normal" life
Hildy tells Walter flat out she is done:
> *"Walter, you wouldn't know what it means to want to be respectable and live a halfway normal life. The point is, I'm through."*
— segs 455–457, **[10:34–10:44]**
*(Frame verified: Hildy in striped suit facing Walter in the newspaper office, [10:43].)*

### 2. With whom — she's marrying **Bruce Baldwin**
She is engaged to Bruce Baldwin, an insurance man she met in Bermuda:
> *"He's in the insurance business."* — seg 464, **[10:56]**
> *"Uh, Baldwin, Bruce Baldwin."* — seg 483, **[11:31]**
> *"You're now talking about the man I'm marrying tomorrow."* — seg 488, **[11:37–11:40]**
*(Frame verified: Hildy leaning back toward Walter as she reveals Bruce's name, [11:32].)*

Bruce wants a traditional domestic life — *"He wants a home and children"* (seg 480, **[11:26–11:27]**) — and Hildy says he treats her like a woman, not an errand boy (segs 473–474, **[11:13–11:17]**).

### 3. Destination — **Albany, New York**, that same day
They are leaving on the afternoon sleeper train:
> *"Well, Albany's a mighty good insurance town."* — seg 689, **[17:02–17:04]**
> *"Well, we're leaving today at four o'clock, taking the sleeper for Albany."* — segs 727–728, **[17:57–18:00]**
*(Frame verified: Walter and Bruce at the restaurant table discussing the departure, [17:59].)*

---

**Summary:** Hildy plans to leave journalism, marry insurance man Bruce Baldwin, and move to Albany — settling into the respectable domestic life (home, children, normalcy) she tells Walter she has always wanted.

baseline.answer: Hildy plans to **get married and leave the newspaper business entirely** [586.2-591.7], moving to **Albany** [981.9-982.2] with her fiancé **Bruce Baldwin** [691.0-692.7], an insurance man she met in Bermuda [648.9-649.3]. She intends to "be a woman" and live a normal domestic life [606.5-608.6], traveling with Bruce and his mother on the six o'clock train [1553.1-1555.5].

rows.question: How does Walter Burns repeatedly get Bruce Baldwin arrested, and for what?

cutroom.answer:
I have all the evidence I need. Here is the complete, sourced answer.

---

## How Walter Burns Gets Bruce Baldwin Arrested (Three Times)

This is *His Girl Friday* (1940). Walter orchestrates three separate arrests to keep Bruce away from Hildy and delay their departure.

---

### Arrest 1 — Stolen Watch [40:55]
**The scheme:** Walter's crook-for-hire, **Diamond Louis**, plants a stolen watch on Bruce and then turns around and accuses him of theft. A police officer named Mike confirms Bruce cannot be released because "he's accused of stealing a watch, and they found the watch on him" — and names Diamond Louis as the accuser.

> **seg 1568** [40:51] *"I never stole a watch in my own life."*
> **seg 1572** [40:56] *"He's accused of stealing a watch, and they found the watch on him."*
> **seg 1576** [41:01] *"Diamond Louis, the biggest crook in town."*

Bruce himself half-suspects Walter but talks himself out of it: *"About being arrested at first, I thought maybe Walter Burns might have something to do with it. But then, of course, I realized he couldn't have."* (**seg 1591** [41:24])

**Frame verified:** [40:55] — Bruce is visible behind jail bars; Hildy is at the cell door arguing with the guard. ✔

---

### Arrest 2 — Mashing [51:52]
**The scheme:** Walter instructs a contact ("Vanjie") to go to the taxi cab in front of the Criminal Courts Building where Bruce is waiting. The woman — described as "very blonde" — fakes a dizzy spell. When Bruce tries to help her, he is arrested for mashing.

Walter sets it up on-screen:
> **seg 1813–1814** [50:31] *"There's a guy waiting in a taxi in front of the criminal court's building. His name is Bruce Baldwin."*
> **seg 1817** [50:38] *"Ralph Bellamy. Oh, him? Can you handle it?"*
> **seg 1818** [50:40] *"I've never fought on you yet, have I?"*

Bruce's account when Hildy reaches him:
> **seg 1852** [51:51] *"Arrested again. What for this time?"*
> **seg 1854** [51:56] *"Mashing."*
> **seg 1857–1859** [52:02] *"I was sitting right in the taxi where you left me and the young lady seemed to have a dizzy spell and I just…"*
> **seg 1864–1865** [52:44] *"Yeah, she's a blonde. Yes, very blonde."*

Hildy's instant reaction: *"Never mind, I know how it happened."* (**seg 1866** [52:19]) — she immediately calls Walter Burns (**seg 1867**).

**Frame verified:** [51:56] — Bruce is at a police booking counter on the phone; a **blonde woman** stands immediately to his left; a uniformed officer stands behind them. ✔

---

### Arrest 3 — Counterfeit Money [89:10]
**The scheme:** Walter has Diamond Louis supply **$450 in counterfeit bills** and sends them to Hildy via Louis as expense money. Hildy (unknowingly) passes the bills to Bruce as travel money for the trip to Albany. Bruce is pulled in at the 4th Precinct station.

Walter arranges the counterfeit money on-screen:
> **seg 1823** [50:52] *"Louis, I need $450 worth of counterfeit money."*
> **seg 1827–1828** [50:59] *"Well, take it over to Hilly. Hello, it's coming right over. Yeah, I'm sending it over with Louis."*

The arrest call to Hildy:
> **seg 2669** [89:09] *"What for? For having counterfeit money?"*
> **seg 2670** [89:10] *"Well, where did you get it? [You] gave it to me."* — Bruce tells Hildy she gave it to him (which Walter gave her).

Hildy's final damning admission to Walter at the film's end:
> **seg 2674** [89:50] *"I didn't know you had him locked up."*

**Frame verified:** [89:10] — Hildy and Walter are both on phones in the newsroom, confirming the call about Bruce's third arrest. ✔ [89:50] — Walter leans over the desk as Hildy confronts him about keeping Bruce "locked up." ✔

---

### Summary Table

| # | Charge | Walter's Method | Key Segments | Timestamp |
|---|--------|----------------|--------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Stolen watch** | Diamond Louis plants watch on Bruce, then accuses him | seg 1568, 1572, 1576, 1591 | [40:51–41:28] |
| 2 | **Mashing** | Sends a blonde woman to feign a dizzy spell in Bruce's taxi | seg 1813–1818, 1852–1866 | [50:31–51:56] |
| 3 | **Counterfeit money** | Has Louis supply fake $450; Hildy unknowingly passes it to Bruce | seg 1823–1828, 2669–2670, 2674 | [50:52 / 89:09–89:50] |

Each arrest buys Walter more time to keep Hildy working on the Earl Williams story — and away from the train to Albany with Bruce.

baseline.answer:
Walter engineers **three arrests** of Bruce Baldwin:

1. **Stolen watch** [2456.6–2462.9]: Bruce is charged with stealing a watch that was planted on him by Walter's associate Diamond Louis ("He's accused of stealing a watch, and they found the watch on him…Diamond Louis, the biggest crook in town").

2. **Mashing** [3107.4–3137.1]: While Bruce waits in a taxi, Walter sends a blonde woman (Evangeline) to feign a "dizzy spell" near him, resulting in a mashing charge ("I was sitting right in the taxi where you left me and the young lady seemed to have a dizzy spell").

3. **Counterfeit money** [5349.5–5363.1]: Walter repays Hildy the $450 she spent on a tip using counterfeit bills (supplied by Louis [3052.3–3059.6]), which Bruce ends up carrying, leading to his third arrest ("What for? For having counterfeit money?" / "Well, where did you get it? I gave it to me").

Each arrest is designed to delay Bruce and keep Hildy from leaving on the train to Albany.

rows.question: How is Earl Williams ultimately saved at the end?

cutroom.answer:
Here is a complete, evidence-based account of how Earl Williams is ultimately saved.

---

## How Earl Williams Is Saved

The rescue unfolds in three interlocking steps:

### 1. Hidden in a Roll-Top Desk — [62:16–62:17]
After Williams escapes and finds his way to the pressroom, Hildy Johnson stuffs him into a large roll-top desk to shield him from the police swarming outside.
- **seg 2300** [62:16]: *"Erl, get in this desk."*
- **seg 2405** [1:08:27]: *"Now that he's inside the desk… that desk is crawling with cops out."*
- **Frame at [62:17]** ✓ — verified: shows Hildy urgently directing someone toward the large desk in the pressroom.
- When police eventually force the desk open, Williams is found unconscious inside (**seg 2592** [83:04]: *"Boys don't try any funny stuff — Williams was unconscious when they opened the desk"*).

### 2. The Governor's Reprieve — Suppressed, Then Exposed — [55:24–57:06]
The governor had already signed a reprieve on the grounds that Williams is insane. A messenger named **Pettybone** delivered it to Mayor/Sheriff Hartwell:
- **seg 1958** [55:24]: *"That's from the governor."*
- **seg 1959** [55:25]: *"It was a reprieve for Earl Williams."*

But Hartwell immediately suppresses it — he bribes Pettybone to deny ever delivering the document, dangling a cushy job at the city sealer's office (**segs 2003–2048** [55:36–57:06]: *"You never arrived with this… you'd like to make $350 a month… city sealer's office"*).

### 3. Pettybone Produces the Reprieve — Mayor Forced to Back Down — [1:23:33–1:26:39]
Pettybone reappears at the pressroom, reprieve in hand. Walter Burns confronts the mayor in front of witnesses:
- **seg 2614** [1:24:45]: *"Here's your reprieve, Shade."*
- **seg 2628** [1:25:59]: *"This document is authentic and Williams has been reprieved."*
- **seg 2629** [1:26:09]: The mayor capitulates — *"take those handcuffs off."*
- **seg 2638** [1:26:18]: Pettybone is escorted to the warden's office: *"We'll take you over to the warden's office and deliver this reprieve."*
- **Frame at [86:03]** ✓ — verified: shows Pettybone (center, document in hand) alongside a broadly smiling Walter Burns — the exact moment the authentic reprieve is pressed on the authorities.

---

### Summary
Earl Williams is saved by a **convergence of two things**: Hildy (and Walter) buying time by concealing him in a roll-top desk, and the belated delivery of the **governor's legitimate reprieve** — which Hartwell had bribed Pettybone to bury. Walter Burns forces Pettybone back into the open, the reprieve is declared authentic on the spot (**seg 2628**, [86:03]), the handcuffs come off, and Pettybone walks the document to the warden's office, permanently halting the execution.

baseline.answer:
Earl Williams is ultimately saved through the governor's **reprieve**, which the sheriff had been trying to suppress. A messenger named Pettibone had delivered the reprieve earlier [55:17–55:30], but the sheriff attempted to bribe him to make it disappear [1:03:00–1:03:51].

Walter Burns then dramatically produces the reprieve document [1:24:51], and after Pettibone confirms his story, it is declared authentic:

> *"This document is authentic and Earl Williams has been reprieved"* [1:26:00]

The sheriff is exposed for attempting to conceal the reprieve and hang Williams for political reasons (to win the election), and the hanging is called off.
